title: US hurtling toward population decline even as Americans say they want bigger families, new report warns | Institute for Family Studies
gist: A Fox News writeup of the IFS report warns the US could face population decline within decades not because Americans reject families, but because actual fertility (below 1.6) has fallen far below the ~2.4 children they still say they want.

insights.claim: Americans still say they want roughly 2.4 children while actual fertility has fallen below 1.6 per woman, described as one of the widest gaps between desired and actual family size in modern history.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: The gap frames low fertility as unmet demand rather than changed preferences, implying policy upside if barriers are removed.
insights.locator: Americans still say they want to have roughly 2.4 children

insights.claim: IFS warns the US could be headed for population decline within the next few decades unless current trends change.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Reinforces a near-term demographic peak as a mainstream-media narrative, raising political salience of pronatal policy.
insights.locator: The Institute for Family Studies (IFS) is warning

insights.claim: Lyman Stone, director of the IFS Pronatalism Initiative, ties the fertility discrepancy closely to family formation rather than a decline in desire for children.
insights.speaker: Institute for Family Studies
insights.so_what: Points to marriage and partnering dynamics as the binding constraint on fertility, directing where interventions should aim.
insights.locator: Lyman Stone, director of IFS' Pronatalism Initiative
